title: NIS2 Supply Chain Security Assessment Coverage
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel/competent-authority review.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.25` output, Dependabot reconciliation worksheet, and signed lookup version hash stored with the NIS2 supplier risk evidence bundle.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(d) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.25: NIS2 Supply Chain Security Assessment Coverage.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.25' running on nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv, index=github (Dependabot alerts), index=itsm, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Populate the lookup with a tier-1 supplier whose `next_assessment_date` is seven days in the past and ensure the GitHub subsearch returns twelve open `dependabot_alert` events; the combined row appears with `overdue=1` and `open_supply_chain_alerts` above the threshold.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Use the same supplier row but refresh `next_assessment_date` to ninety days ahead and reduce Dependabot fixtures below three open alerts; the scheduled search produces zero rows and the audit log records a clean run.

dataSources: `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` lookup (supplier_id, supplier_name, tier, next_assessment_date, supplier_code_repo); `index=github` `sourcetype=dependabot_alert` (alert_number, repo, severity); `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:task` for linked remediation tasks when Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)) pulls `/now/table/sn_task`.
app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)), Splunk Add-on for GitHub ([Splunkbase 5596](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5596)) with REST or webhook ingestion for Dependabot.
spl:
| inputlookup nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv
| eval assessment_due=strptime(next_assessment_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval overdue=if(now()>assessment_due,1,0)
| join supplier_code_repo type=left [
    search index=github sourcetype="dependabot_alert" earliest=-30d
    | stats dc(alert_number) as open_supply_chain_alerts by repo
    | rename repo as supplier_code_repo
]
| fillnull value=0 open_supply_chain_alerts
| where overdue=1 OR open_supply_chain_alerts > 10
| table supplier_name, tier, next_assessment_date, overdue, open_supply_chain_alerts
description: Combines the authoritative supplier assessment register with open Dependabot-style repository alerts so procurement sees both paperwork gaps and concrete open dependency findings per supplier code base.
value: Leadership receives one numbered list that pairs "assessment overdue" with "dependency alerts still open" so board updates do not claim green third-party posture while GitHub still shows dozens of unfixed issues.
implementation:
(1) Publish `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` under `lookups/` with `supplier_id` matching your CMDB key. (2) Map `supplier_code_repo` to GitHub `repo` names (`org/name`). (3) Configure GitHub TA (5596) modular input or HEC for Dependabot JSON. (4) Optional: mirror ServiceNow tasks (1928) to prove compensating documentation exists. (5) Tune threshold `open_supply_chain_alerts > 10` per tier.

**Note:** Normalize `supplier_code_repo` to lowercase `org/repo` to match GitHub `repo` before scheduling.
visualization: Table (supplier_name, tier, overdue flag, alert count), bar chart by tier, single value overdue tier-1 count.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Assessment due date mis-keyed in CSV** — Distinguish: typo pushes next_assessment_date into past. Suppress: governance process to fix lookup; interim `where supplier_name!="EXAMPLE_VENDOR"` filter.
• **Dependabot duplicates same CVE across alerts** — Distinguish: `dc(alert_number)` still inflates counts. Suppress: replace with `dc(security_advisory_id)` after field extract.
• **Supplier uses GitLab not GitHub** — Distinguish: repo field empty, join misses. Suppress: add parallel subsearch for `sourcetype=gitlab:vulnerability` or mark supplier `sca_platform=github` in register.
• **Planned acceptance of low-severity dependency noise** — Distinguish: risk acceptance memo signed for dev-only repos. Suppress: `vuln_exception_register.csv` keyed on repo with `max_cvss`.
• **Quarterly questionnaire cadence mis-aligned with CSV monthly updates** — Distinguish: real-world review happened but CSV stale <30 days. Suppress: sync ServiceNow field `u_next_assessment` nightly via scripted export.
equipmentModels: GitHub Advanced Security (Dependabot alerts, code scanning, secret scanning)
GitLab Ultimate (optional — dependency scanning via sourcetype gitlab:vulnerability)
Splunk Add-on for GitHub (Splunkbase 5596, Dependabot alert ingestion via REST or webhook)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, task/incident tracking for remediation evidence)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This UC is the second supply chain UC alongside UC-22.2.16. While UC-22.2.16 monitors real-time data flows to suppliers (network-level), this UC monitors the software supply chain — specifically, whether supplier-related code repositories have unresolved dependency vulnerabilities (Dependabot alerts) and whether formal third-party risk assessments are current. The combination addresses two distinct aspects of Art.21(2)(d): (1) assessment of suppliers' security practices (questionnaires, audits, certifications), and (2) technical verification of supplier-provided or supplier-maintained software components (dependency scanning).
• The supplier assessment register: `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` with columns: `supplier_id` (unique identifier matching your procurement/CMDB system), `supplier_name` (human-readable name), `tier` (critical/high/medium/low — based on the supplier's access to your systems and data), `next_assessment_date` (ISO 8601 date — when the next formal risk assessment is due), `supplier_code_repo` (GitHub repository in `org/repo` format — the repository containing code provided by or maintained in collaboration with this supplier), `assessment_type` (questionnaire/audit/certification/penetration_test — the method used for the last assessment), `certifications` (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, etc. — current certifications held by the supplier). Not every supplier has a code repository — for suppliers without software deliverables, `supplier_code_repo` should be null and the Dependabot join will return zero alerts (acceptable).
• GitHub Dependabot alerts feed: configure the Splunk Add-on for GitHub (Splunkbase 5596) to ingest Dependabot alerts from your organisation's repositories. Two ingestion methods:
  (a) **REST API polling**: the TA queries `api.github.com/orgs/{org}/dependabot/alerts` (or per-repo: `api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/dependabot/alerts`) at a configured interval. The GitHub personal access token (PAT) or GitHub App must have `security_events` scope. Set polling interval to 900 seconds (15 minutes) for near-real-time alerting.
  (b) **Webhook push**: configure a GitHub webhook on the organisation (Settings > Webhooks > Add webhook) with events `dependabot_alert` and `code_scanning_alert`. Point the webhook to a Splunk HEC endpoint. This is lower-latency but requires inbound network access from GitHub to your Splunk HEC.
• GitHub token permissions: the token needs `repo` scope (to list repositories) and `security_events` scope (to read Dependabot alerts). For GitHub Enterprise Server, the API URL changes to `https://github.example.com/api/v3/orgs/{org}/dependabot/alerts`. Rotate tokens every 90 days. Store the token in Splunk's credential store (Apps > GitHub TA > Configuration > Account), never in plaintext configuration files.
• Understanding Dependabot alert structure: each Dependabot alert has: `number` (unique alert identifier per repo), `state` (open, dismissed, fixed, auto_dismissed), `security_advisory.ghsa_id` (the GitHub Security Advisory ID), `security_advisory.severity` (critical, high, medium, low), `dependency.package.name` (the vulnerable package), `dependency.manifest_path` (the file declaring the dependency — package.json, requirements.txt, pom.xml, etc.), `created_at` (when the alert was first created). The SPL uses `dc(alert_number)` to count unique open alerts per repo.
• ServiceNow integration (optional but recommended): Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) configured to pull task or incident records from `sn_task` or `incident` tables. This provides evidence that overdue assessments or high Dependabot alert counts have triggered remediation workflows — proving not just that you detected the gap, but that you acted on it.
• Index requirements: `index=github` for Dependabot alerts, retention ≥ 90 days. `index=itsm` for ServiceNow remediation tasks, retention ≥ 365 days. `index=audit_evidence` ≥ 7 years for compliance evidence.
• RBAC: role `nis2_supply_chain_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = github, itsm, audit_evidence` and access to the supplier assessment lookup. GitHub Dependabot alerts may contain vulnerability details for your organisation's code — restrict access to the security and GRC teams.
• Delineation from UC-22.2.16: UC-22.2.16 monitors network-level data flows to suppliers (bytes transferred, access patterns). This UC monitors the software supply chain (code dependencies, assessment governance). Together they provide comprehensive Art.21(2)(d) coverage. An organisation deploying both UCs can demonstrate to regulators that they monitor suppliers at both the network and software levels.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**GitHub Dependabot alerts:**
Install the Splunk Add-on for GitHub (Splunkbase 5596). Navigate to Apps > GitHub TA > Configuration > Account. Provide:
- GitHub API URL: `https://api.github.com` (or your GHES URL)
- Personal Access Token or GitHub App credentials
- Scopes: `repo`, `security_events`

Enable the Dependabot alert input:
- Input type: Dependabot Alerts (or REST API input pointing to the alerts endpoint)
- Organisation: your GitHub org name
- Interval: 900 seconds
- Index: `github`
- Sourcetype: `dependabot_alert`

If the TA does not have a native Dependabot input, configure a custom REST input or HEC webhook:
```ini
[http://github_dependabot]
token = <HEC-TOKEN>
index = github
sourcetype = dependabot_alert
```
For webhook: in GitHub > Organisation Settings > Webhooks, add a webhook pointing to `https://hec.splunk.example.com:8088/services/collector` with Content-Type `application/json`, events: `dependabot_alert`.

Verify the feed:
```
index=github sourcetype=dependabot_alert earliest=-7d
| stats count dc(number) as alerts dc(repository.full_name) as repos
| table count alerts repos
```
Expected: `alerts` should approximate the total open Dependabot alerts across your organisation. `repos` should match the number of repositories with Dependabot enabled. If `count=0`, check:
- Token permissions: `curl -H "Authorization: token <PAT>" https://api.github.com/orgs/<org>/dependabot/alerts?per_page=5`. If 403, the token lacks `security_events` scope.
- TA logs: `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd source="*github*" ERROR`.
- Webhook delivery: GitHub > Organisation Settings > Webhooks > Recent Deliveries. Check for failed deliveries (HTTP status != 200).

**Field mapping verification:**
```
index=github sourcetype=dependabot_alert state=open earliest=-7d
| head 10
| table number, repository.full_name, security_advisory.severity, dependency.package.name, created_at
```
The SPL uses `alert_number` (not `number`) and `repo` (not `repository.full_name`). If the raw field names differ, create field aliases:
```ini
[dependabot_alert]
FIELDALIAS-alert_number = number AS alert_number
FIELDALIAS-repo = repository.full_name AS repo
FIELDALIAS-severity = security_advisory.severity AS severity
```

**Supplier assessment register deployment:**
Place `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-supply-chain/lookups/`. Define in `transforms.conf`:
```ini
[nis2_supplier_assessment_register]
filename = nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
```
Validate: `| inputlookup nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv | stats count dc(supplier_id) as suppliers dc(tier) as tiers`. Verify `suppliers` matches your third-party inventory.

**Repository name alignment check:**
The critical join key is `supplier_code_repo` matching GitHub's `repo` field. GitHub uses `org/repo-name` format (all lowercase). Verify:
```
| inputlookup nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv
| where isnotnull(supplier_code_repo)
| eval repo_lower=lower(supplier_code_repo)
| stats count(eval(supplier_code_repo!=repo_lower)) as case_mismatches
```
If `case_mismatches > 0`, normalise the CSV to lowercase. Also verify against GitHub: `index=github sourcetype=dependabot_alert | stats dc(repo) as repos values(repo) as repo_list`. Compare `repo_list` with `supplier_code_repo` values.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.25 NIS2 Supply Chain Assessment Coverage`.

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **`inputlookup nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` as the driver**: the search starts from the supplier register, not from GitHub. This ensures every supplier appears in the results — including those without code repositories (where the Dependabot join returns null). The register is the authoritative source for the supplier universe.

• **`strptime(next_assessment_date, "%Y-%m-%d")` and `overdue` flag**: converts the text date to epoch and compares with `now()`. The `overdue` field is a simple binary: 1 if the assessment is past due, 0 if not. This is the governance dimension of the UC.

• **`join supplier_code_repo type=left`**: left join ensures suppliers without code repositories still appear (with `open_supply_chain_alerts=0` after `fillnull`). The subsearch filters to the last 30 days of Dependabot alerts and counts unique alert numbers per repository. The 30-day window means the search shows the current state of open alerts, not historical ones that were already fixed.

• **`dc(alert_number) as open_supply_chain_alerts`**: distinct count of alert numbers avoids double-counting if the same alert appears in multiple webhook deliveries or API polling cycles. However, this counts ALL open alerts regardless of severity. For a more nuanced approach, add severity filtering: `where state="open" AND severity IN ("critical","high")`.

• **`where overdue=1 OR open_supply_chain_alerts > 10`**: two independent conditions:
  1. Assessment overdue — governance gap. The supplier's formal risk assessment has not been completed by the due date.
  2. More than 10 open Dependabot alerts — technical gap. The supplier's code repository has a significant backlog of known vulnerable dependencies.
  Either condition alone is an audit finding. Both together indicate a supplier that is both governance-neglected and technically exposed — the highest risk category.

The threshold of 10 is a starting point. Active open-source projects may have 20+ Dependabot alerts at any time (many low-severity). Tune based on your organisation's risk appetite: critical-tier suppliers might trigger at 5 alerts, while low-tier suppliers trigger at 20.

Extended search — severity-weighted alert scoring:
```spl
| join supplier_code_repo type=left [
    search index=github sourcetype=dependabot_alert state=open earliest=-30d
    | eval severity_weight=case(severity="critical",10, severity="high",5, severity="medium",2, severity="low",1, 1=1, 0)
    | stats sum(severity_weight) as weighted_risk dc(alert_number) as open_supply_chain_alerts by repo
    | rename repo as supplier_code_repo
]
| fillnull value=0 open_supply_chain_alerts weighted_risk
| where overdue=1 OR weighted_risk > 50
```
This weights critical alerts 10x and high alerts 5x, creating a composite risk score that prioritises suppliers with severe vulnerabilities over those with many low-severity findings.

Extended search — ServiceNow remediation evidence:
Append a check for whether remediation tasks exist:
```spl
| join supplier_id type=left [
    search index=itsm sourcetype=snow:task assignment_group="*vendor*" OR assignment_group="*supplier*" state!=7 earliest=-90d
    | stats count as open_remediation_tasks latest(short_description) as latest_task by u_supplier_id
    | rename u_supplier_id as supplier_id
]
| fillnull value=0 open_remediation_tasks
```
This shows whether the organisation has created ServiceNow tasks to address the gaps identified by this UC — proving not just detection but response.

Schedule: cron `0 6 * * 1` (weekly, Monday at 06:00). Time range: use the lookback windows embedded in the subsearch (`earliest=-30d` for Dependabot). Trigger: `Number of results > 0`.

Alert actions: (1) For `tier=critical` and `overdue=1`: PagerDuty page to the CISO. (2) For any tier with `open_supply_chain_alerts > 20`: email to the development security team with the repository name and alert count. (3) Write all results to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:supply_chain:assessment`.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Assessment overdue parity**: select a supplier with `overdue=1`. Verify in the `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` that `next_assessment_date` is indeed past. Cross-check with the GRC tool or procurement system — has the assessment actually been completed but the CSV not updated? If so, this is a process gap in CSV maintenance.

(b) **Dependabot count parity**: select a supplier with `open_supply_chain_alerts > 0`. Navigate to GitHub > Repository > Security > Dependabot alerts. Filter by state `open`. Count the alerts. Compare with the Splunk result. They should match. If Splunk shows fewer, the TA may be filtering by severity or state during ingestion — check the TA's input configuration.

(c) **Repository name match test**: run `| inputlookup nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv | where isnotnull(supplier_code_repo) | join supplier_code_repo type=inner [search index=github sourcetype=dependabot_alert | stats count by repo | rename repo as supplier_code_repo] | stats count as matched`. Compare `matched` with the number of suppliers that have code repositories. If `matched` is lower, repository names don't align — normalise to lowercase `org/repo` format.

(d) **Negative test**: select a supplier with a recent assessment date (within 30 days) and a code repository with fewer than 5 open alerts. Verify they do NOT appear in the results.

(e) **Supplier without code repository test**: add a supplier to the register with `supplier_code_repo` set to null (e.g., a consulting firm with no code deliverables). Set `next_assessment_date` to 60 days ago. Run the search. Verify the supplier appears with `overdue=1` and `open_supply_chain_alerts=0` — proving the search surfaces governance gaps even without software supply chain data.

(f) **Severity distribution check**: run `index=github sourcetype=dependabot_alert state=open | stats count by severity | sort severity`. Verify the distribution makes sense — typically, low and medium alerts dominate. If 90% are critical, investigate whether the severity mapping is correct.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Supply Chain Assessment Coverage`):
• **Row 1 — Headline tiles**: Single-value `Suppliers with overdue assessments` (red ≥ 1 for critical/high tier). Single-value `Total open Dependabot alerts (all suppliers)` (sum). Single-value `Tier-1 suppliers not assessed this year` (count). Single-value `Assessment coverage %` (assessed within cycle / total).
• **Row 2 — Risk matrix**: scatter plot with `overdue` days on the X-axis and `open_supply_chain_alerts` on the Y-axis, sized by `tier` (critical = large dot). Suppliers in the upper-right quadrant (overdue AND many alerts) are the highest risk.
• **Row 3 — Assessment calendar**: table of all suppliers with `supplier_name`, `tier`, `next_assessment_date`, `overdue`, `assessment_type`, `certifications`. Colour-code by urgency: green (not overdue, < 10 alerts), amber (overdue OR > 10 alerts), red (overdue AND > 10 alerts).
• **Row 4 — Top vulnerable repositories**: bar chart of `open_supply_chain_alerts` by `supplier_code_repo`, top 15. Annotate with `supplier_name` and `tier`.
• **Row 5 — Remediation tracking**: if ServiceNow integration is configured, show `open_remediation_tasks` per supplier. Suppliers with findings but no tasks indicate a response gap.

Runbook (owner: Procurement / Development Security):
1. Weekly (Monday): review alert. For each triggered row:
   - `overdue=1`: contact procurement. Determine whether the assessment is genuinely overdue or the register hasn't been updated. If overdue: schedule the assessment immediately. For critical-tier suppliers, the assessment must be completed within 30 days or the CISO must approve a documented risk acceptance.
   - `open_supply_chain_alerts > 10`: contact the development team responsible for the repository. Determine whether the alerts represent genuine risk or are low-severity/dev-only dependencies. For critical/high severity alerts: create a ServiceNow incident for remediation. For medium/low: batch into a sprint backlog item.
2. After a supplier assessment is completed: update `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` with the new `next_assessment_date` (typically today + assessment cycle days). Update `certifications` if new certs were presented.
3. Quarterly: review the overall assessment coverage. Target: 100% of critical-tier suppliers assessed within cycle, 90% of high-tier, 80% of medium-tier. Present to the board risk committee.
4. After a major supply chain vulnerability disclosure (e.g., a new Log4j): run `index=github sourcetype=dependabot_alert dependency.package.name="*log4j*" state=open | stats count by repo`. Identify all affected supplier repositories and escalate immediately.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Join yields zero Dependabot alerts — all `open_supply_chain_alerts=0`.** The `supplier_code_repo` field in the CSV doesn't match the GitHub `repo` field. GitHub uses lowercase `org/repo-name` format. Fix: normalise both sides: `| eval supplier_code_repo=lower(supplier_code_repo)` in the outer search and `| eval repo=lower(repo)` in the subsearch. Also verify the org name: some organisations use a hyphenated org name in GitHub (`my-org`) but a different format in the register (`MyOrg`).

• **GitHub API returns 401 Unauthorized.** The PAT has expired or been revoked. Fix: regenerate the token in GitHub > Settings > Developer settings > Personal access tokens. Update the token in the TA's credential store. GitHub fine-grained PATs expire after their configured lifetime (default 30 days for fine-grained tokens). Classic tokens do not expire unless revoked, but fine-grained tokens are recommended for security.

• **Dependabot alert count includes dismissed alerts.** The subsearch should filter `state=open` but if the field name differs (e.g., `alert_state` or `dismissed_at IS NULL`), dismissed alerts may be counted. Fix: `index=github sourcetype=dependabot_alert | stats count by state | sort state` to verify available state values. Filter explicitly: `where state="open"`.

• **Subsearch hits result limit.** Splunk's subsearch has a default `maxout` of 10,000 results. If your organisation has more than 10,000 open Dependabot alerts, the subsearch truncates and some repositories' counts will be missing. Fix: increase `maxout` in `limits.conf` (requires change control on the search head): `[subsearch] maxout = 50000`. Alternatively, restructure the search to avoid subsearch: use `| append` + `| stats` instead of `join`.

• **CSV `strptime` returns null for `next_assessment_date`.** The date format doesn't match `%Y-%m-%d`. Common issues: (a) European format `15/06/2025` — fix: `strptime(next_assessment_date, "%d/%m/%Y")`. (b) Date with time `2025-06-15T00:00:00` — fix: `strptime(next_assessment_date, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")`. (c) Empty value for suppliers without a scheduled assessment — this is valid; `overdue` will be 0 (since `now() > null` evaluates to false).

• **Supplier uses GitLab not GitHub.** If some suppliers maintain code in GitLab instead of GitHub, the Dependabot subsearch will not find their repositories. Fix: add a parallel subsearch for GitLab dependency scanning: `| append [search index=gitlab sourcetype=gitlab:vulnerability scanner_type=dependency_scanning state=detected earliest=-30d | stats dc(id) as open_supply_chain_alerts by project_path | rename project_path as supplier_code_repo]`. Add a `sca_platform` column to the register (github/gitlab/bitbucket) and route to the appropriate subsearch.

• **Mass false positives after GitHub Dependabot auto-triage changes.** GitHub periodically updates its advisory database and may re-open previously dismissed alerts or create new alerts for existing dependencies. This causes sudden spikes in `open_supply_chain_alerts`. Fix: filter by `created_at` to focus on alerts older than 7 days (recent alerts may be triaged within the week): `where state="open" AND strptime(created_at,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ") < relative_time(now(),"-7d")`.
grandmaExplanation: We line up the paperwork deadlines for checking outside suppliers with the real open problems reported in the code libraries those suppliers help maintain, so nobody thinks the homework is done when the toolbox is still messy.
